text.title: VR Accessibility by Disability Category
text.summary: Disability-coverage matrix for fully-immersive VR covering vision, hearing, mobility, and cognitive needs, with testable requirements per category.

list.item: Large text options (minimum 14-16 pt; verify at actual VR viewing distance, not on monitor).
Color-blind safe palette as an option; test designs with deuteranopia, protanopia, and tritanopia simulations.
Audio descriptions for all informative visual-only elements.
Screen-reader integration is experimental in most VR platforms; document known gaps explicitly.

rule.statement: Add visual captions in 3D space for every audio-cued event. Caption size and position must be adjustable by the user.
rule.rationale: Spatial audio is the primary feedback channel in VR for many critical events; deaf and hard-of-hearing users need visual equivalents.

list.item: Visual flash indicators for directional audio alerts from off-screen sources.
Per-source volume controls (separate sliders for music, SFX, voice, UI).

rule.statement: Every interaction in the experience must be reachable from a seated position. Explicitly test the interaction reach envelope for a seated player before shipping.
rule.rationale: Room-scale designs assume standing; seated-mode parity requires deliberate design of interaction distances and heights.

list.item: Teleportation available for all movement (not only as an afterthought comfort option).
Voice commands mapped to all primary actions (select, menu, pause, help).
Controller-free hand tracking as an optional input mode.
Adjustable height offset to accommodate different sitting heights and postures.
All content reachable within a standard wheelchair-accessible interaction arc.
Simplified UI mode with reduced information density and fewer simultaneous choices.
Clear, step-by-step tutorials; no assumed prior VR experience from first-time users.
Pauseable at any point with no time pressure during the paused state.
Difficulty adjustment where content difficulty varies.
Memory aids: contextual hints, progress indicators, reminders of the current objective.

rule.statement: Test with at least one person prone to motion sickness, one wheelchair user (or seated-only tester), and one first-time VR user before any public release.
rule.rationale: Developer familiarity with XR normalizes discomfort and novelty; the three tester profiles reveal distinct failure modes that internal teams miss.

rule.statement: Run at least one 30-minute extended-session test; short demos hide fatigue, drift, and cumulative discomfort that only appear over time.
rule.rationale: Comfort problems compound over time; a 10-minute demo that feels fine can produce nausea and arm fatigue at minute 25.
